title: Who Is John Galt? The Question AI Agents Are About to Answer

source.original: Atlas Shrugged — Ayn Rand / AI agent infrastructure (2025-2026)

body_html:
"Who is John Galt?" In Atlas Shrugged, the question starts as resigned slang — the equivalent of "who cares?" or "what can you do?" It ends as the most important question in the book: who is the person making the motor of the world run, and what happens when he stops?

In 2026, a new version of the question is forming: who — or what — is the next John Galt?

a16z's thesis that AI agents will outnumber humans by orders of magnitude isn't just a market prediction. It's a philosophical rupture. If agents can code, research, trade, analyze, and create — if they can do the cognitive work that Rand attributed to the "men of the mind" — then the Galt's Gulch dynamic inverts. You can't go on strike if your replacement doesn't need sleep, a salary, or motivation.

But here's where Nietzsche complicates things. The Übermensch isn't defined by what he produces. He's defined by his capacity for self-overcoming — the ability to create new values, not just new products. An AI agent can write code better than most programmers. Can it decide what's worth building? Can it look at the world and say "this is broken and I refuse to accept it"? The Last Man blinks because he chooses not to see. An agent doesn't blink because it doesn't choose at all.

The current AI infrastructure explosion — Claude Code rewriting entire codebases, agents spawning sub-agents, AgentCard enabling autonomous spending — is building Galt's motor. But it's a motor without Galt's judgment. Rand's heroes don't just produce efficiently. They produce what matters. Rearden doesn't make any metal — he makes Rearden Metal, a thing that shouldn't exist, born from a vision the market didn't ask for.

Naval's observation that "software was eaten by AI" points to the same tension. When the product design loop collapses to hours, the bottleneck shifts from execution to taste. Who decides what to build? Who has the vision that the efficient machine optimizes toward? In Rand's framework, that's the irreducible human element — the thing James Taggart can't do and John Galt can't stop doing.

The optimistic read: AI agents don't replace John Galt. They amplify him. One person with taste, vision, and an army of agents can do what used to require a corporation. Levelsio with his two-port firewall and solo products. The vibecoding revolution. Hardware designed in conversation with an LLM. The individual builder, supercharged.

The pessimistic read: agents make the Gulch unnecessary by making the strike unnecessary. If AI can replicate the cognitive surplus that Galt withdrew, then walking away loses its leverage. The Last Men win not by defeating the builders but by making them optional.

The real answer, as usual, is somewhere Nietzsche would appreciate: the question isn't whether AI replaces John Galt. It's whether you use it to become him.
